coredata.dc:title: Efficient conversion of bio-waste lignin into high-value fluorescent nitrogen-modified carbon quantum dots for live-cell imaging
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ren, Haitao
Labidi, Abdelkader
Gao, Ting
Padervand, Mohsen
Liang, Xinxin
Wang, Chuanyi
coredata.dc:description: Sustainable and green natural lignin raw materials are considered as reliable precursors for large-scale production of carbon quantum dots (CQDs). However, extracting CQDs with ultra-low cytotoxicity and favorable fluorescence properties from lignin remains a challenge. Herein, the bio-waste lignin was converted into crude CQDs by an eco-friendly and rapid microwave pyrolysis approach in just 15 min. The as-obtained CQDs modified with nitrogen-atoms (N-CQDs) display a representative graphite-like structure, strong hydrophilicity, tiny particle size of 6.9 nm, and bright green fluorescence with a quantum yield of approximately 22.2% and fluorescence lifetime of 4.29 ns. Moreover, the N-CQDs exhibit high fluorescence stability under the conditions of a range of pH (5–11), continuous UV irradiation (∼2 h), and NaCl presence with concentrations of 0–1.2 mol/L. More importantly, the N-CQDs show negligible cytotoxicity. Even at the higher N-CQD concentration (300 μg/mL), A549 cells still maintain over 90% activity. The N-CQDs could be utilized for fluorescence imaging in living cells based on the above favorable characteristics. The present work paves the way for the value-added utilization of bio-waste lignin and the production of safe cell imaging agents.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bio-waste lignin
Microwave conversion
Carbon quantum dot
Surface modification
Biological imaging

Cellular toxicity evaluation of the obtained N-CQDs
Structural analysis of the produced N-CQDs
The optical behavior of the synthesized N-CQDs
PL stability of the prepared N-CQDs
Cytotoxicity and fluorescence imaging of the developed N-CQDs in living cells

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52161145409), and SAFEA of China (\u201CBelt and Road\u201D Innovative Exchange Foreign Expert Project, DL2023041004L). Calculation of quantum yield; Raman spectrum of N-CQDs; Normalized PL spectra of N-CQDs; Excitation and emission spectra of N-CQDs; Appearance photos of N-CQDs solution.

This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52161145409), and SAFEA of China (\u201CBelt and Road\u201D Innovative Exchange Foreign Expert Project, DL2023041004L).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, (b) high-resolution TEM image, (c) size distribution histogram, and (d) the XRD pattern of the N-CQDs.
(a) FT-IR spectrum of N-CQDs. (b) XPS full spectrum of N-CQDs and (c) corresponding XPS element content distribution pie chart. (d) C1s spectrum, (e) O1s spectrum, and (f) N1s spectrum of N-CQDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of N-CQDs in water. (b) Photographs of N-CQDs suspension under sunlight and 400 nm irradiation (300 W Xe, QD400). (c) CIE coordinates of the N-CQDs. (d) Excitation-dependent PL spectra and (e) corresponding 3D PL spectra of N-CQDs (the excitation wavelengths are 300–500 nm with an interval of 10 nm). (f) Fluorescence lifetime curve of N-CQDs.
(a) The effect of pH, (b) different concentrations of NaCl, (c) irradiation time with a 365 nm UV-lamp on the PL intensity of N-CQDs (λex = 405 nm).

(a) A549 cell viability following 24 or 48 h incubation at 37 oC with various N-CQDs concentrations. (b-c) Images of A549 cells stained with 200 μg/mL N-CQDs for 24 h were taken using confocal fluorescence microscopy at various resolutions.
Extraction process of the N-modified CQDs from bio-waste lignin by a microwave pyrolysis strategy and their use in bioimaging.
The properties of unmodified CQDs derived from lignin.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The characteristics of N-modified CQDs derived from lignin.

head.ce:title: Efficient conversion of bio-waste lignin into high-value fluorescent nitrogen-modified carbon quantum dots for live-cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Xi'an 710021, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Xi'an 710021, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Maragheh, P.O. Box 55181-83111, Maragheh, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Maragheh

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Maragheh, P.O Box 55181-83111, Maragheh, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sustainable and green natural lignin raw materials are considered as reliable precursors for large-scale production of carbon quantum dots (CQDs). However, extracting CQDs with ultra-low cytotoxicity and favorable fluorescence properties from lignin remains a challenge. Herein, the bio-waste lignin was converted into crude CQDs by an eco-friendly and rapid microwave pyrolysis approach in just 15 min. The as-obtained CQDs modified with nitrogen-atoms (N-CQDs) display a representative graphite-like structure, strong hydrophilicity, tiny particle size of 6.9 nm, and bright green fluorescence with a quantum yield of approximately 22.2% and fluorescence lifetime of 4.29 ns. Moreover, the N-CQDs exhibit high fluorescence stability under the conditions of a range of pH (5–11), continuous UV irradiation (∼2 h), and NaCl presence with concentrations of 0–1.2 mol/L. More importantly, the N-CQDs show negligible cytotoxicity. Even at the higher N-CQD concentration (300 μg/mL), A549 cells still maintain over 90% activity. The N-CQDs could be utilized for fluorescence imaging in living cells based on the above favorable characteristics. The present work paves the way for the value-added utilization of bio-waste lignin and the production of safe cell imaging agents.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The global annual lignin output is around 70 million tons, of which only approximately 5% is efficiently utilized, and the remaining approximately 95% exists predominantly as a waste byproduct of the paper-pulp sector and cellulosic ethanol biorefinery (Gao et al., 2021; Balakshin et al., 2021; Luo, Abu-Omar, 2018). This not only pollutes the environment severely, but also wastes such valuable resources. Therefore, green and high-value-added application of bio-waste lignin is imminent. Massive amounts of bio-waste lignin should be transformed into value-added products in the context of energy scarcity and the steady depletion of non-renewable resources. Lignin, the only type of aromatic biomass found in nature (Sadeghifar, Ragauskas, 2022; Qiu et al., 2023), is regarded as a promising carbon source for the sustainable and low-cost production of carbonaceous nanomaterials. CQDs, as an emerging kind of carbon-based nanomaterial, have attracted considerable interest due to their fascinating photoluminescence (PL) properties, fine size, ultra-low toxicity, favorable biocompatibility, facile surface modification, and high hydrophilicity (Jayakumar et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2023). They have been intensively utilized in several domains (i.e., pollutant detection, photo/electrocatalysis, anti-counterfeiting, photoelectric devices, biological imaging, cancer therapy, and so on.) (Liu et al., 2020; Li et al., 2021; Pang et al., 2022). Compared to other CQDs precursors, bio-waste lignin is a promising candidate to prompt research on green and mass manufacturing of high-value CQDs. Besides, bio-waste lignin transformation ensures the successful recycling and reuse of waste resources and, to some extent, environmental pollution and the usage of dangerous and expensive chemicals.

After releasing the first report on preparing lignin-based CQDs in 2016 (Chen et al., 2016), several sources of lignin have been employed for fabricating the CQDs, including enzymatic kraft lignin (Pei et al., 2021), alkali lignin (Shi et al., 2019), lignosulfonate lignin (Rai et al., 2017), and hydrolysis lignin (Xu et al., 2020). To generate CQDs from lignin, hydrothermal/solvothermal methods have been typically used, which are generally performed at 120–200 °C of temperature for 12–20 h. Zhu et al. (Zhu et al., 2021) used a two-step hydrothermal process under optimized conditions (200 ℃, 16) to produce CQDs from lignin and o-amino benzene sulfonic acid. Zhu et al. (Zhu et al., 2022) utilized a unique two-stage method to produce N-doped graphene quantum dots from alkali lignin, initiating with an oxidative cleavage step (refluxing HNO3 for 8 h) and ending with a hydrothermal step (200 ℃, 12 h). Meanwhile, Gao et al. (Gao et al., 2019) made blue CQDs by hydrothermally treating lignin for 12 h at 180 °C in a high-acid environment (H2SO4 and HNO3). In comparison to the extensively reported inorganic quantum dots (e.g., CdS, CdSe, SnS, and CdTe) with either low hydrophilicity, poor stability, or high toxicity (Wareing et al., 2021; Caires et al., 2020; Wagner et al., 2019), lignin-based CQDs show unique advantages such as high water-solubility, favorable stability, low toxicity, and low cost, indicating that such lignin-derived CQDs are a suitable alternative for the hazardous metal-based semiconductor quantum dots, particularly in the biological imaging applications. Nonetheless, the reported quantum yields (QY) of unmodified lignin-based CQDs are commonly less than 18% and emission wavelengths in the blue light region ( Table 1), which limits their broad application to some extent, such as biological imaging. Therefore, modification of lignin-derived CQDs towards enhanced their properties is desired. Nitrogen modification is an effective and traditional tactic for regulating the surface structure of CQDs, which can enhance their PL properties and expand their potential applications, as listed in Table 2. Unfortunately, synthesizing the nitrogen-modified CQDs derived from lignin through hydrothermal/solvothermal methods often faces several issues: (1) requiring long reaction time leading to low synthesis efficiency; (2) using strong acids which can have harmful effects on the environment and human health; and (3) relatively high cytotoxicity limiting their application in biological imaging. As a result, creating a high-efficiency and acid-free strategy for producing CQDs from bio-waste lignin with ultra-low cytotoxicity and favorable fluorescence properties still remains challenging.

Here, we report a nitrogen-modified CQDs (N-CQDs) with ultra-low toxicity and favorable fluorescence properties, which were prepared from bio-waste lignin via a rapid microwave pyrolysis approach within 15 min. The structure and optical properties of lignin-based N-CQDs were studied in detail by diverse characterization techniques. The obtained N-CQDs exhibit excitation-dependent green fluorescence with a QY of 22.2%, which is superior compared to most reported lignin-based CQDs fabricated from direct hydrothermal. Furthermore, the N-CQDs with ultra-low toxicity and high hydrophily were effectively employed in the biological imaging of a living cell model system, revealing its actual biological potential in the future. These findings provide an effective strategy to produce lignin-based CQDs with desirable properties for cancer cell imaging applications.

Macklin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) supplied polyethyleneimine (PEI, M.W. 1800). Aladdin Industrial Corp. (Shanghai, China) provided the alkali lignin and Rhodamine B. Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (China) supplied 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazoyl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT, 98%). Shanghai Saibaikang Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (China) provided human lung adenocarcinoma cell lines (A549) for this work. All reagents utilized in this study were of analytical quality and were not purified further.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fast and cleaner production of N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The N-CQDs were prepared utilizing a fast and environmentally friendly microwave-assisted pyrolysis technique ( Scheme 1). First, 0.2 g of alkali lignin and 40 mL of ultrapure water were vigorously mixed with 0.5 mL of Polyethyleneimine (PEI) to form a uniform mixture. The resulting mixture was then poured into a 100 mL glass beaker and cooked for 15 min in a household microwave oven (800 W). After completion of the reaction, the obtained viscous product was dissolved in ultrapure water and filtered using a 50 nm membrane to eliminate the residues. Then, the obtained transparent suspension was further dialyzed with a 2000 D dialysis bag for 24 h, and the water was displaced every 6 h. Afterward, the yellow-brown N-CQDs solution was collected and dried to generate powdered N-CQDs. Similar to the above steps, CQDs were synthesized only without adding PEI. During this process, high-quality N-CQDs were created using bio-waste lignin, a plentiful and low-cost natural material, without utilizing any hazardous agents. The synthesized CQDs with microwave heating have various advantages, including ease of operation, low cost, great efficiency, and low energy use. The entire manufacturing process is efficient and environmentally friendly, and fully meets the standards of sustainable production, providing some essential guidance for the green and added-value usage of bio-waste lignin. Additionally, the product yield of N-CQDs was determined 39.28% in terms of the following formula：
where YN-CQDs represent the yield of N-CQDs, W N-CQDs denote the weight of N-CQDs, and Wr refers to the weight of raw materials.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) was performed on a FEI Talos F200i field emission electron microscope operating at a voltage of 200 kV. Raman spectrum was recorded on a HORIBA LabRAM HR Evolution Raman Spectrometer excited with a 532 nm laser. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was carried out in the KBr medium on a Brucker (Verte70 V) spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded on a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha spectrometer using Al Kα X-ray beam of 250 W, as the excitation source. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was obtained using a powder X-ray diffractometer (D8 ADVANCE, Bruker, Germany) in the range of 10° to 80°. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were measured using a UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The PL spectra were acquired using an Edinburgh Instruments FS5 spectrofluorometer; lifetime measurements were also performed on FS5 Edinburgh Instruments equipped with an EPLED-340 pulsed source.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular toxicity evaluation of the obtained N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The MTT technique was utilized to assess the toxicity of the as-synthesized N-CQDs. Moreover, A549 cells were cultivated on a 96-well plate as an object. The cells were cultured in a mixture containing diverse concentrations of N-CQDs (0, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, and 300 μg/mL) for 24 and 48 h (5.0% CO2, 37 oC). Six parallel tests were run on each N-CQDs concentration to calculate experimental errors. Following that, the incubation medium was filtered out, and 100 L of the MTT solution (0.5 mg/mL) was injected into the well and hatched for another 4.0 h at 37 °C. Then, the supernatant was superseded with 100 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide, followed by 15 min of wobbling. Using an enzyme labeling device, the optical density (OD) of the well was determined at 490 nm. The cell viability (%) was calculated as follows:
where ODtreated and ODcontrol represent the OD of the well in the existence of N-CQDs and blank, respectively.

Fluorescence imaging of A549 cells was used to explore the potential of N-CQDs in biolabeling. Concisely, A549 cells were transplanted into Dulbecco's modified eagle solution containing 10% (pH 7.0) phosphate-buffered saline (5.0% CO2, 37 oC). The cells were then cultured for 24 h in a 5% CO2 atmosphere with 200 μg/mL N-CQDs solution. To remove N-CQDs, before the cells were fixed with 4.0% formaldehyde, they were rinsed three times with phosphate-buffered saline. Under 405 nm excitation, the fluorescence images were obtained using a confocal microscope (Leica TCS SP8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural analysis of the produced N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The N-CQDs were extracted from lignin by microwave strategy using PEI as a modifier. The microstructure and chemical composition of the N-CQDs were investigated by combining various characterization techniques. As illustrated in Fig. 1a, the as-prepared N-CQDs are spherical, monodispersed, and well isolated from one another. The high-resolution TEM image revealed that the N-CQDs have a favorable crystallization nature with a lattice distance of 0.21 nm (Fig. 1b), which corresponds to the graphitic carbon (100) plane (Zhang et al., 2023; Ren et al., 2023). The histogram in Fig. 1c depicts the size distribution of the products. The statistical diameter of the N-CQDs is of size ranging from 3.5 to 10.5 nm with a mean size of 6.9 nm. Furthermore, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the N-CQDs (Fig. 1d) displays a wide peak around 2θ=21.3°, which is consistent with the (002) lattice distance of carbonaceous nanomaterials with some defects (Ding et al., 2018). Furthermore, the D band originates from the disordered carbon and the G band originates from the graphite carbon (Li et al., 2023), which are ascribed to the two bands at 1558 and 1334 cm-1 in the Raman spectra of N-CQDs (Fig. S1), respectively. The intensity ratio of I
D to I
G is 0.42, indicating that N-CQDs have a high level of graphitization. This outcome is in line with the discussion of high-resolution TEM image in Fig. 2b. These findings demonstrate the graphitic-like structure of the N-CQDs, containing some defects, produced by microwave-aided pyrolysis from bio-waste lignin.

The FT-IR spectrum was used to identify the surface groups of N-CQDs. As presented in Fig. 2a, two peaks at 3356 and 3275 cm-1 correspond to the stretching vibration modes of the O-H and N-H bonds, respectively (Ren et al., 2023). The obvious absorption bands at 1651, 1548, 1466, 1303, and 1040 cm-1 are associated with the CO, CC, C-N, C-O-C, and C-OH bonds, respectively (Wang et al., 2022). Additionally, the bands at 2941 and 2840 cm-1 represent the stretching vibrations of O-H. These findings suggest that N and O-containing groups are widely anchored on the surface of N-CQDs. Moreover, the chemical constituents and surface states of the produced N-CQDs were explored by employing an XPS technique. The XPS full spectrum of N-CQDs is shown in Fig. 2b, and it represents three characteristic peaks at 284.8, 531.1, and 400.1 eV, which are corresponded to C1s, O1s, and N1s, respectively. The amounts of C, O and N atoms obtained through XPS are 65.6%, 24.5% and 9.9%, respectively, as seen in Fig. 2c. Four distinct peaks can be observed in the C1s spectrum of N-CQDs (Fig. 2d) which are attributed to the presence of various carbon bonds, including CC/C-C, C-O, C-N, and CO (Liu et al., 2022; Zhu et al., 2021). This result indicates that the prepared N-CQDs have been successfully modified by N atoms. Additionally, the O1s spectrum of N-CQDs (Fig. 2e) shows two evident peaks at 531.2 and 532.8 eV that match with CO and C-OH/C-O-C bonds (Abdelhameed et al., 2023). The N1s spectrum of the N-CQDs (Fig. 2f) indicates three characteristic peaks at 399.3, 400.3, and 401.8 eV, which are ascribed to C-N-C, N-(C)3, and N-H bonds, respectively (Zhao et al., 2020). The XPS and FT-IR results indicate the presence of O and N-containing groups such as -COOH, -OH, and -NH2 on N-CQDs’ surface, which improves the stability and hydrophilicity of N-CQDs in aqueous systems, thereby promoting their application in cellular imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optical behavior of the synthesized N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: UV–vis and PL spectra were used to explore the optical properties of the produced N-CQDs. As demonstrated in Fig. 3a, two different absorption peaks arise at 225 and 282 nm, which are ascribed to π-π* transition of the conjugated CC and n-π* transition of the aromatic CO, respectively (Kurniawan et al., 2022). Besides, the N-CQDs exhibit significant absorption in the visible region of 380–580 nm, which may be caused by the presence of the N atoms. Their modification not only offers wide absorption in the UV-Vis region but also has favorable fluorescence properties, which is crucial for N-CQDs applications. In Fig. 3a, the excitation spectrum, monitored by 508 nm emission, consists of a broad peak with a maximum of 405 nm, which results from band gap transition. In Fig. 3b, the N-CQDs suspension displays a yellow-brown color under sunlight, while showing a bright green fluorescence under 400 nm light irradiation. In Fig. 3c, the green fluorescence was further validated by the International Commission on Illumination (CIE) coordinates at (0.2771, 0.4086) under emission data of 405 nm excitation. In comparison to blue light emission N-CQDs, the as-obtained green N-CQDs offer higher tissue penetration and may have considerable potential in cell imaging.

Furthermore, Fig. 3d shows the PL spectra of N-CQDs suspension at diverse excitation wavelengths. When the excitation wavelengths are adjusted from 300 to 500 nm, the PL emission peak increases from 460 to 570 nm. The greatest emission at 508 nm is attained with an excitation wavelength of 405 nm. The corresponding normalized emission spectra (Fig. S2) show the redshift clearly at increasing excitation wavelengths. Like most fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, these N-CQDs also exhibit an excitation-dependent PL behavior. This characteristic PL property might be due to the diverse surface defect states of N-CQDs (Tang et al., 2022; Siddique et al., 2021). The 3D fluorescence contour map (Fig. 3e) of the N-CQDs shows numerous contours (corresponding to the core and surface) emerging at the same emission center, 508 nm. This outcome further demonstrates the favorable green emission of the produced N-CQDs.

The fluorescence decay curve of N-CQDs is shown in Fig. 3f. This curve was well-fitted via a double exponential model which can be given as follows (Yang et al., 2021):
R
t
=
α
1
exp
− t
τ
1
+
α
2
exp
− t
τ
2
where the fractional contributions of two transition channels to PL emission of N-CQDs, corresponding to decay lifetimes τ1 and τ2, are represented by α1 and α2. According to the realized fitting, the average fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs was determined to be 4.29 ns, indicating that the synthesized N-CQDs are best suited for biological imaging applications (Ramanan et al., 2014). Specifically, the τ1 of N-CQDs is 0.91 ns with a percentage of 29.31%, while the τ2 of N-CQDs is 5.69 ns with a percentage of 70.69%. Furthermore, τ1 is associated with the eigenstate transition from the core states of N-CQDs, whereas τ2 is associated with the non-radiation transition from the surface states of N-CQDs (Li et al., 2014; Tang et al., 2021). Evidently, the PL emission channel of N-CQDs is mainly induced by τ2, which is associated with their surface defect states. This implies that the fluorescence property of N-CQDs mainly originates from τ2 with the surface defect states. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. S3, after introducing nitrogen into CQDs, their excitation and emission spectra are significantly enhanced and redshifted, demonstrating that the enhanced fluorescence of N-CQDs belongs to surface defect states to a large extent. Notably, in Figs S4 and S5, the fluorescence QY of N-CQDs was calculated to be about 22.2% by a reference method, which is larger than that of CQDs without N modification (7.3%). As shown in Tables 1 and 2, the QY of the produced N-CQDs is comparable to most previously reported lignin-based CQDs. More importantly, they have a relatively long emission wavelength, short synthesis time, abundant surface groups, and eco-friendliness, which would endow the synthesized N-CQDs with better biological imaging applications.
NA: Not Available

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL stability of the prepared N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: It is crucial to study PL stability under various complex conditions for practical applications of the N-CQDs. The effect of pH, ionic strength, and illumination time on the PL intensity of N-CQDs suspension was explored. As presented in Fig. 4a, the obtained N-CQDs exhibit favorable PL intensity over a wide pH ranging from 5 to 11, which is an advantageous attribution for a fluorescent probe to be utilized in biological systems, such as bioimaging, biosensing, and drug delivery (Wang et al., 2022). The PL intensity of N-CQDs is almost unaffected by NaCl concentrations up to 1.2 mol/L (Fig. 4b), indicating that N-CQDs have remarkable stability under high salt ions. After 120 min of UV irradiation, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs suspension almost does not decay (Fig. 4c), while exhibiting bright fluorescence (Fig. S6), indicating that the prepared N-CQDs have strong resistance to photobleaching. Furthermore, after approximately 90 days of storage, the N-CQDs suspension does not aggregate and maintains a bright blue-green fluorescence under 365 nm UV light (Fig. S7), which further supports the long-term storage stability of the N-CQDs suspension. The above-mentioned favorable results demonstrate that as-prepared N-CQDs have numerous advantages as fluorescent nanomaterials for practical application in biological imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and fluorescence imaging of the developed N-CQDs in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: To validate whether the N-CQDs have low cytotoxicity, as well as its suitability for biological applications, such as live-cell imaging, the cytotoxicity of synthesized N-CQDs at various concentrations (from 0 to 300 μg/mL) was evaluated through the MTT assay using the A549 cell line as a sample. As presented in Fig. 5a, there is no remarkable decrease in cell viability after treatment with the N-CQDs for 24 or 48 h, A549 cells still maintain a survival rate of about 90% at the concentration of N-CQDs up to 300 μg/mL, providing evidence that the N-CQDs are nontoxic to cells. As shown in Tables 1 and 2, the cytotoxicity of the synthesized N-CQDs is lower compared to previous research results. Generally, the concentration of N-CQDs used in cytophagocytosis is under 1000 μg/mL (Zhou et al., 2021; Yue et al., 2022). Therefore, the N-CQDs may have great potential for cell imaging and even animal in vivo imaging. The results reveal the excellent biocompatibility of the N-CQDs derived from bio-waste lignin, which made them suitable fluorescent probes for biological imaging applications.

As demonstrated above by the systematic analysis, the N-CQDs exhibited ultra-low cytotoxicity, favorable fluorescence properties, nanosecond fluorescence lifetime, low photobleaching properties, fine size, long-term fluorescence stability, and high hydrophily. These are the desired properties that may make N-CQDs a promising fluorescent nano-sized candidate for cellular imaging applications. To attest N-CQDs feasibility in this pathway, we performed confocal imaging of A549 cells treated with 0.2 mg/mL N-CQDs. As displayed in Figs. 5b-5c, the bright green fluorescence on the cell cytoplasmic area of A549 cells was observed, which signifies that the N-CQDs can infiltrate the cell through its membrane and maintain their fluorescent behavior in the cellular environment. The N-CQDs are easily internalized into cells through endocytosis due to their hydrophilic nature and the existence of functional groups on their surface (Raveendran, Kizhakayil, 2021). Moreover, the A549 cells treated with N-CQDs show no significant change in shape, confirming their low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility. These phenomena demonstrate that the N-CQDs derived from bio-waste lignin can be utilized as an attractive fluorescence probe in biological imaging.

In summary, bio-waste lignin was transformed into value-added N-CQDs with ultra-low toxicity through a rapid microwave pyrolysis approach. Compared with reported lignin-based CQDs, as-synthesized N-CQDs exhibit ultra-low cytotoxicity, and even at higher concentrations of N-CQD (300 μg/mL), A549 cells still keep 90% viability. Moreover, the obtained N-CQDs have a strong green fluorescence with a favorable QY of about 22.2% and fluorescence lifetime of 4.29 ns. The presence of O and N functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs improves their fluorescent performance and hydrophilicity. The N-CQDs exhibit favorable fluorescence stability throughout a wide pH ranging from 5 to 11, long-term UV exposure, and high ionic strengths. Considering their ultra-low cytotoxicity and favorable fluorescent characteristics, the N-CQDs offer a prospective application in cellular imaging, as demonstrated in the in vitro (A549 cells culture) model system. This study provides an appealing pathway for the value-added exploitation of bio-waste lignin by converting it into ultra-low cytotoxicity fluorescent N-CQDs for live cell imaging.

Although our strategy has significant advantages in synthesizing high-quality N-CQDs, the product yield of the obtained N-CQDs is relatively low (ca. 39.28%), which undoubtedly limits the large-scale production of CQDs nanomaterials. Increasing the product yield of CQDs will be a target of the future study.

Chuanyi Wang: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Haitao Ren: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Abdelkader Labidi: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Ting Gao: Validation, Software. Mohsen Padervand: Writing – original draft. Xinxin Liang: Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52161145409), and SAFEA of China (“Belt and Road” Innovative Exchange Foreign Expert Project, DL2023041004L).

ce:section.ce:para: Calculation of quantum yield; Raman spectrum of N-CQDs; Normalized PL spectra of N-CQDs; Excitation and emission spectra of N-CQDs; Appearance photos of N-CQDs solution.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.indcrop.2024.118832.
